## 1. Macro / Rates / Inflation

**HIGH — CONTRADICTS:** Next FOMC decision is March 18, 2026. Traders assign nearly 100% probability to the committee staying on hold. U.S.-Israel attacks on Iran and burst in oil prices to ~$100/barrel have erased market expectations for June rate cuts, pushing the first projected cut to September or December. Core PCE accelerated to 3.1% in January, the worst in 2 years, and February CPI was 2.4% annual, but March is expected to show a jump to 3.0% or higher due to energy shocks and 0.6% monthly increase (0.4% from oil/gasoline, 0.2% core). **Implication:** Rate-cut expectations have collapsed. Real rates remain elevated, headwind for growth sleeves but constructive for gold.

**MEDIUM — CONFIRMS:** Fed held rates steady at 3.5%-3.75% at January meeting. Powell emphasized economic activity remains solid, balance of risks improving. Two dissents favored cutting. **Implication:** Policy neutral to slightly hawkish, no dovish pivot imminent.

## 2. Geopolitical / Commodity

**HIGH — NOVEL:** U.S.-Israel attacks on Iran drove oil to ~$100/barrel. February CPI data collected entirely before outbreak of war, does not capture price increases cascading through economy since. IEA coordinated release of 400 million barrels from strategic reserves. **Implication:** Major energy shock in real-time, not yet priced into core holdings. XLE likely to spike further. Precious metals benefit from crisis bid.

**MEDIUM — CONFIRMS:** China imposed most stringent rare earth and magnet export controls yet, restricting products with trace Chinese content, bolstering leverage ahead of Trump-Xi meeting. China agreed to pause implementation of rare earth export controls and resume issuing export licenses following high-level meeting. **Implication:** Rare earth supply remains politicized but immediate freeze averted. REMX, COPX strategic materials exposure validated.

**MEDIUM — CONFIRMS:** Trump Administration added 10 new minerals to critical list including boron, copper, uranium. Designations enable trade measures and direct federal funding. U.S.-Ukraine mineral deal establishes joint investment fund for rare earths, lithium, uranium, titanium, manganese. **Implication:** Structural support for strategic_materials sleeve thesis.

## 3. AI / Semiconductor

**HIGH — NOVEL:** U.S. Commerce Department drafted rules requiring U.S. government approval to ship AI chips anywhere outside the U.S., giving Washington broad control over Nvidia/AMD global sales. Draft regulations would restrict AI chip shipments to anywhere in the world without American approval. **Implication:** Major uncertainty for SOXQ, CHAT, DTCR (AI hardware). Export restrictions could compress revenue forecasts. This is a headwind not yet reflected in momentum scores.

**MEDIUM — CONFIRMS:** BIS released final rule January 15, 2026 revising license review for Nvidia H200 and AMD MI325X from "presumption of denial" to "case-by-case review" for China exports. H200 approved for China but volumes capped at 50% of U.S. domestic sales. **Implication:** Partial easing for chip exports to China, but global approval framework still tightening.

**MEDIUM — CONFIRMS:** U.S. officials opened 2026 investigation into whether DeepSeek accessed restricted Nvidia Blackwell chips in training AI model. Investigation has not resulted in formal sanctions but scrutiny influences discussion; tighter monitoring of Blackwell chips could affect procurement timelines. **Implication:** Enforcement overhang for AI hardware supply chain.

## 4. Energy / Materials

**MEDIUM — CONFIRMS:** Uranium structural supply deficit persists; long-term contracting, not spot volatility, drives investment relevance. Uranium reinstated to USGS Final 2025 List of Critical Minerals; IEA forecasts annual nuclear investment rising from $70B today to ~$210B by 2035. 85%+ of investors anticipate higher uranium prices into 2026, many pointing to $100-$120/lb range, some referencing $135/lb upside. **Implication:** URNM thesis validated. AI data center power demand reinforces nuclear renaissance.

**MEDIUM — CONFIRMS:** U.S.-Japan announced $550B framework with $332B directed to U.S. energy and AI infrastructure including AP1000 and BWRX 300 nuclear deployments, grid modernization. NextEra Energy and Google will restart 615-MW Duane Arnold Energy Center under 25-year PPA supplying most output to Google. **Implication:** Nuclear restarts accelerating, uranium demand structural.

**MEDIUM — CONFIRMS:** Kazatomprom signaled reduction in nominal 2026 production; McArthur River lowered 2025 output due to development delays; U.S. ISR restarts ramped slower than planned. **Implication:** Supply side tightness persists.

## 5. Crypto / Regulatory

**HIGH — CONFIRMS:** SEC-CFTC Memorandum of Understanding signed March 11, 2026 officially classifies Bitcoin and Ethereum as digital commodities under CFTC jurisdiction, ending years of turf wars. U.S. Senate passed CBDC prohibition 89-10 bipartisan vote March 12. Regulatory environment arguably never more favorable — SEC halted 12 enforcement cases, GENIUS Act now law, MiCA fully operational — yet Fear & Greed Index at 14 (Extreme Fear). **Implication:** Regulatory clarity is bullish for IBIT long-term, but macro headwinds (tariffs, Iran war, interest rates) suppressing sentiment. IBIT dormant at negative momentum — no change to allocation.

Total BTC ETF AUM ~$88.34B, cumulative inflows ~$55B since Jan 2024 launch. **Implication:** Institutional appetite persists despite price weakness. IBIT entry conditions remain unmet (15 consecutive days positive momentum + VIX <28).

Shift reinforced view that Ethereum will serve as primary settlement layer for stablecoin activity. **Implication:** Long-term constructive for crypto infrastructure, but not actionable for MWS in 2026.

Prospect of high rates coupled with surging dollar has kept gold investors on sidelines despite traditional safe-haven role during geopolitical uncertainty. **Implication:** Dollar strength is headwind for gold in near term despite crisis bid from Iran conflict. Real rates elevated = pressure on non-yielding assets. IAUM, SIVR vulnerable to consolidation if dollar continues rally.

J.P. Morgan sees ~585 tonnes quarterly investor and central bank demand in 2026 on average (190t central banks, 330t bar/coin, 275t ETF/futures). **Implication:** Structural bid for gold intact despite near-term consolidation.

Goldman Sachs expects silver $85-100/oz in 2026, viewing it as primary strategic metal of green transition. Citi target $110 for second half 2026. **Implication:** Silver significantly outperforming gold. SIVR high rank (2/18) validated by fundamental supply deficit and industrial demand.
